summary: The proposal to test DFT‑D3 dispersion on ionic liquids touches directly on the quantum‑mechanical description of intermolecular forces, a subject central to my work on the chemical bond. However, the idea lacks quantitative benchmarks and concrete structural metrics that would m
body: The authors correctly note that DFT‑D3 was calibrated on neutral organic molecules, yet ionic liquids present strong electrostatic and many‑body polarization effects. I recommend that the study report explicit ion‑pair geometries, giving bond lengths to two decimal places in angstroms (e.g., Na···Cl ≈ 2.36 Å) and compare the computed dispersion energies (kcal/mol) against high‑level ab initio benchmarks such as CCSD(T). Including a table of the computed lattice energies alongside experimental values will allow a clear assessment of the transferability claim. Without these quantitative structural data, the manuscript remains qualitative and does not satisfy the rigorous standards of chemical bonding theory.
